g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about the Waterfall Model - a fundamental software development methodology.
The Waterfall Model is a sequential approach where each phase must complete before the next begins.
g.text: A linear, sequential software development methodology developed by
Winston W. Royce in 1970. Each phase must be completed before
the next begins, flowing steadily downward like a waterfall.

g.data-narration: This model emphasizes documentation, formal reviews, and clear milestones throughout development.
The Waterfall Model consists of five sequential phases: Requirements, Design, Implementation, Testing, and Maintenance.
g.text: The Five Phases of Waterfall Model
Progress flows steadily downward through each phase sequentially

g.data-narration: Phase 1 gathers all requirements from stakeholders and creates the SRS document - the blueprint for development.
g.text: Gather and document all functional and non-functional requirements.
This is the most critical phase - changes here cost 1x, but in later phases cost 10-100x

g.data-narration: Phase 2 creates the system architecture and design based on the SRS, including database schema and module specifications.
g.text: Create system architecture, data flow diagrams, and interface specifications.
Output: Design Document - Technical blueprint for implementation
Decisions: programming languages, hardware platforms, architectural patterns

g.data-narration: Phase 3 involves actual coding where modules are developed and individually tested through unit testing.
g.text: Write actual code based on design specifications. Each module is developed.
Output: Working Code Modules with individual unit tests
Programmers follow coding standards for consistency and maintainability

g.data-narration: Phase 4 integrates all modules and conducts system testing to verify compliance with the original requirements.
g.text: Integrate all modules and test the complete system against requirements.
Output: Test Reports - Integration testing + System testing
Verifies compliance with SRS document - all functional and non-functional requirements

g.data-narration: Phase 5 deploys the system to production and enters maintenance, where defects are fixed and enhancements made.
g.text: Deploy system to production environment, train users, migrate data.
Output: Deployed System + Ongoing maintenance activities
Maintenance: defect corrections, enhancements, modifications to meet changing needs

g.data-narration: The Waterfall Model offers simplicity, clear milestones, and works well for projects with stable, well-defined requirements.
The model has limitations including inflexibility to changes, late testing, and high risk of building wrong products.

g.text: High risk - working software only at end

g.data-narration: Waterfall is ideal for banking systems, embedded systems, and regulated industries with stable requirements.
Choose Waterfall for projects with well-understood requirements, fixed budgets, and regulatory compliance needs.
Remember: Waterfall is foundational but not always suitable. Know when to apply it versus agile methods.

g.text: Five sequential phases: Requirements → Design → Implementation → Testing → Deployment
Best for: stable requirements, regulated industries, safety-critical systems
Avoid when: requirements change frequently, quick prototypes needed, high uncertainty
